w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex wp14

w:r.w:t: Help Validate Chronoacupuncture for Jet Lag
and meal scheduling show variable efficacy

postulated that stimulating horary points based on destination time zones during travel could help pre-synchronize organ systems to the destination time zone, targeting jet lag's circadian misalignment. Theory and anecdotal evidence say this works. Unfortunately, no studies have validated it. We aim to address that lack of evidence with an app that provides a destination
based 24-hour schedule of acupuncture points for
travelers to stimulate as they travel
The scientific literature describes how c
ircadian rhythms synchronize physiological processes to a 24-26 hour cycle
the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN), a cluster of neurons entrained by light.
The SCN coordinates peripheral clocks in organs like the liver and heart
lock genes drive a molecular feedback loop, producing rhythmic gene expression.
external signals such as light, temperature, activity, and meals.
when rapid time zone changes desynchronize these
that acupuncture significantly affects circadian biology, demonstrating measurable impacts on circadian clock genes, neurotransmitter regulation, and sleep-wake cycles

While these studies employed acupuncture points known to assist with sleep, they establish the neurobiological plausibility of acupuncture serving as a non-photic zeitgeber for circadian rhythm modulation.
the horary points on each meridian,
Our solution was to use alternative channel points to replace “inaccessible” points understanding that
For practicality and ease of location, w

SP-10 (above the knee) for SP-3, BL-2 (inner eyebrow) for BL-66, GB-20 (back of head) for GB-41, LIV-8 (medial knee) for LIV-1, and KI-27 (upper chest) for KI-10. In TCM theory, any point on a meridian can affect the entire pathway
and this substitution maintains meridian specificity.
Detailed instructions are provided on the website
app is entirely free with no
advertisements. As an incentive, participants who use the app for a valid journey and complete the survey will enjoy free lifetime access.
Once you have the app installed, follow the simple directions
If you have questions, email info@jetlagpro.com.
The reminders are persistent and remain on the user's phone (or
image beside a looping video showing how to stimulate the point.
After the trip ends, the app
for 2 days later. This survey is a streamlined assessment based on the Liverpool Jet Lag Questionnaire's core symptom domains.
compare survey results to baseline severity data from Waterhouse et al.'s 2007 comprehensive quantitative assessment
grouped by the number of acupressure points
Traditional clinical trials face significant challenges in jet lag research
not undermine validity because the expected sample size of
will provide sufficient statistical power to detect meaningful effects.
Waterhouse, J., Reilly, T., Atkinson, G., & Edwards, B. (2007). Jet lag: Trends and coping strategies. The Lancet, 369(9567), 1117–1129.
Khoe, W.H. (1978). Treatment of jet lag syndrome by acupuncture. American Journal of Acupuncture, 6(2), 135–139.
Amaro, J. (2001). Time Travel: Contemporary Applications for Jet Lag from Ancient Principles. Acupuncture Today, October 2001.
Panda, S. (2022). Foundations of circadian medicine.
The mammalian circadian timing system and the suprachiasmatic nucleus as its pacemaker. Biology, 8(1), 13.
, G.K., & FitzGerald, G.A. (2010). Circadian clocks and vascular function. Circulation Research, 106(5), 833-841.
Hastings, M. H., et al. (2003). A clockwork web: circadian timing in brain and periphery. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 4(8), 649–661.
, H., Takahashi, J. S., Green, C. B., & Zheng, N. (2014). Molecular assembly of the period-cryptochrome circadian transcriptional repressor complex.
, I., & Oster, H. (2019). Differentiating external zeitgeber impact on peripheral circadian clock resetting. Scientific Reports, 9, 20114.
Wu, J., & Zhao, Z. (2024). Acupuncture in circadian rhythm sleep–wake disorders and its potential neurochemical mechanisms. Frontiers in Neuroscience, 18, 1346635.
